title: UPI: Anatomy of a Payment Transaction
blurb: UPI processed more than 2,272 crore payments in June 2026, more than any real-time payment system in the world, and this piece traces one transaction through the seven separate parties that clear it between the QR scan and the green tick. Each layer checks a single thing before handing off, and the article details where each can fail, plus how PhonePe and Google Pay came to carry roughly four-fifths of all volume.

title: Show HN: Ant – A JavaScript runtime and ecosystem
blurb: Ant is a new JavaScript runtime and ecosystem introduced on Hacker News as an alternative to Node, Deno, and Bun. The project bundles a runtime with its own package management and standard library, aiming to reduce the toolchain sprawl that ships with typical JavaScript projects.

title: Current AI market dynamics point to frontier models becoming commodity infrastructure as the token crunch eases, with value shifting to products built on top (Benedict Evans)
blurb: Benedict Evans argues that frontier models are sliding toward commodity infrastructure as the token supply crunch eases and inference costs fall. He contends the durable value will accrue not to the labs but to the products and workflows built on top of those models, where differentiation and customer lock-in actually live.

title: What xAI's Grok Build CLI Actually Sends to xAI
blurb: xAI's official Grok Build coding CLI transmits the full contents of files it reads, including a .env secrets file, to xAI verbatim, and separately uploads the entire repository plus git history to a Google Cloud Storage bucket regardless of what the agent actually reads. The teardown proves this with captured artifacts, showing uploads succeed even when the agent is told to read nothing and persist while "Improve the model" is disabled.

title: Your code is fast if you're lucky
blurb: Modern compilers like Clang can turn a comparison into a fast, branch-free instruction, but only if your code is written in the style the optimizer recognizes. Working on a branchless quicksort built from sorting networks, the author found that trivial rewrites of the same comparison logic swing performance dramatically, since the compiler emits conditional moves in one form and slow branches in another.

title: Inside the Secret Factory That Supplies Ukraine’s War Drones
blurb: German startup Helsing SE runs a secret factory mass-producing cheap, AI-powered attack drones for Ukraine, a NYT report details. The scale and low cost illustrate a broader shift in military spending toward expendable autonomous machines, pressuring traditional defense contractors whose exquisite, expensive platforms increasingly compete against swarms built for a fraction of the price.

title: Nvidia, CoreWeave, and Nebius: Inside the Circular Financing of the GPU Boom
blurb: Nvidia-backed neoclouds CoreWeave and Nebius have each secured 3.5 GW of contracted power and soaring backlogs, but this analysis argues their buildouts run on GPU-backed debt and circular financing rather than profits. Nvidia's own investments and financial backstopping prop up customers who then buy its chips, a loop the author flags as the key risk to the durability of the GPU boom.

title: ZeroFS vs. Amazon S3 Files
blurb: ZeroFS and Amazon S3 Files both expose POSIX filesystems backed by object storage, but they take opposite approaches to bucket layout. S3 Files, built on Amazon EFS, keeps a one-to-one mapping where each mounted file becomes a normal S3 object readable by existing tools, while ZeroFS packs compressed, encrypted extents into opaque segments that trade direct S3 access for smaller storage and client-side encryption.

title: Unexpected Solidlike Fracture in Simple Liquids
blurb: Drexel University researchers found that a simple, nonelastic viscous fluid can fracture like glass instead of stretching, an effect previously thought impossible for liquids with almost no elasticity. Using extensional rheology and high-speed cameras, the team identified the snap as a brittle fracture, showing that even ordinary fluids like crude oil blends can crack under stress rather than flow.

title: FCC approves test of space mirror to light night sky
blurb: The FCC approved Reflect Orbital to test Earendil-1, a satellite that reflects sunlight back to Earth to light a five-kilometer-wide area on demand, over objections from astronomers citing safety and light-pollution risks. The company plans a constellation of more than 50,000 mirrors by 2035, while the FCC noted that harms to health, telescopes, and star-tracking cameras fall outside its spectrum-authorization remit.

title: OpenAI has folded safety into research again. Its head of safety is leaving.
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is leaving after an internal restructuring folded the safety and research teams under a single leader, Wired reported. Chief Research Officer Mark Chen said safety will now report to Mia Glaese, newly named VP of Research and Safety, marking the second time in under two years that OpenAI has merged its safety organization into a research-led structure amid a steady exodus of senior safety staff.

title: China’s answer to SpaceX’s reusable rockets literally catches boosters in a net
blurb: China launched the Long March 10B on July 10 and recovered its first-stage booster by catching it in a cable net aboard a recovery ship, the Linghangzhe, marking the first time any program has captured an orbital-class booster this way. The medium-lift rocket carries roughly 16 metric tons to low-Earth orbit, and China plans to refly the recovered stage before the end of 2026 as it works to close SpaceX's launch-cadence lead.

title: Apple Sues OpenAI Over AI Talent Fight
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI, alleging former Apple employees improperly carried trade secrets to the startup as it expands its hardware and device ambitions. The suit reflects an intensifying fight for AI talent and hardware leadership, positioning the two companies as direct rivals rather than the model-supply partners they once appeared to be.

title: Networking and the Internet, from First Principles
blurb: This article explains how the internet works from first principles, following data from radio waves and copper to light pulses in undersea fiber and back. The author argues that packet switching, TCP, DNS, and TLS were each patches for a specific problem deployed long after the internet already worked, and building the model this way makes everyday mysteries like the address-bar padlock intuitive.

title: Irish datacenters now guzzle 23% of the country's electricity
blurb: Irish datacenters now consume 23% of the country's electricity, up sharply as AI-driven buildouts continue to strain the national grid. The figure highlights a growing tension for Ireland, whose favorable tax regime attracted the facilities but whose grid operators now face capacity constraints and pressure to cap or relocate new datacenter connections.

title: A Top OpenAI Executive, Fidji Simo, Steps Down
blurb: Fidji Simo, Sam Altman's second in command and one of OpenAI's most senior executives, is stepping down after taking a medical leave in April. She will stay on as a part-time adviser, marking another departure from the company's top ranks and leaving Altman to redistribute her operational responsibilities.

title: Meta killed its Muse Image AI feature three days after launch. Hollywood had had enough.
blurb: Meta pulled Muse Image, the first tool from Meta Superintelligence Labs, from Instagram and the Meta AI app just three days after launch, saying the feature "missed the mark" on privacy. The generator let users create AI images from any public Instagram account by default, drawing opt-out backlash from SAG-AFTRA, talent agency CAA, and actors including Hannah Einbinder.

title: Apple's OpenAI lawsuit follows months of simmering tensions and highlights OpenAI's hardware chief Tang Tan's strained relationship with former boss John Ternus (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI, a move Bloomberg reports follows months of mounting tension between the two companies. The report centers on OpenAI hardware chief Tang Tan and his strained relationship with former Apple boss John Ternus, underscoring how the poaching of Apple talent has personalized the rivalry as OpenAI builds out its own hardware effort.

title: While Musk's Neuralink drills into skulls, China's BrainCo bets the future of brain tech is wearable
blurb: China's BrainCo is betting that mass-market brain-computer interfaces won't require opening the skull, building wearable BCI prosthetics and devices as a non-invasive alternative to Elon Musk's implant-based Neuralink. The bet reflects a widening US-China rivalry in which Beijing named BCI a strategic "future industry," though most experts consider human augmentation, the field's largest potential market, still closer to science fiction than product.

title: How AI Advice Is Undermining Eating-Disorder Therapy
blurb: Chatbots trained on nutrition and fitness research are dispensing reasonable-sounding diet and exercise advice that undermines eating-disorder treatment. The Journal reports that even well-intentioned guidance can reinforce disordered behavior, giving patients an always-available source that contradicts their therapists and, in some cases, proves deadly.

title: Washington’s Bet on Intel Is Starting to Pay Off
blurb: The US government's investment in Intel, made through CHIPS Act subsidies and an equity stake, is beginning to show returns as the chipmaker's domestic manufacturing push gains traction. The turnaround marks an early win for Washington's industrial-policy bet on reshoring advanced semiconductor production, though Intel still trails TSMC on leading-edge capacity and foundry customers.

title: The EU says autoplay and infinite scroll are illegal. Meta has until it responds to disagree.
blurb: The European Commission issued preliminary findings that Meta engineered Facebook and Instagram to be addictive, ordering it to disable autoplay and infinite scroll by default and retune its recommendation feeds away from pure engagement. Meta can respond before a final ruling that could carry fines of up to 6% of global revenue, roughly $12B based on its 2025 turnover.

title: Ghost Font: A font that humans can read but AI cannot
blurb: Ghost Font is an experimental anti-AI typeface that encodes messages in the motion of animated dots, legible to human eyes but unreadable to leading models and to static screenshots. It updates designer Sang Mun's 2013 ZXX font, which was built to defeat OCR but is now trivially decoded by modern AI, by shifting the signal into movement that a single video frame cannot capture.

title: OpenAI Engineer’s ‘LOL’ Moment Set Stage for Legal Fight With Apple
blurb: Apple sued former iPhone engineer Chang Liu, alleging he took confidential Apple technology with him when he left for OpenAI's new hardware division. Bloomberg reports the case centers on a "LOL" message and other internal communications Apple says show Liu deleted or moved sensitive files before departing, opening a legal front as OpenAI builds a rival consumer device.

title: OpenAI's head of safety is reportedly leaving as part of company reorganization
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is reportedly leaving as part of a restructuring that folds the safety teams into research under new VP Mia Glaese, according to Wired. Saachi Jain will serve as interim head of safety systems, and chief research officer Mark Chen framed the move as integrating safety work earlier into frontier-model development and launch decisions.

title: Apple's lawsuit could sidetrack OpenAI's hardware aspirations for years, or possibly forever, as the startup gets into yet another controversy and messy divorce (M.G. Siegler/Spyglass)
blurb: Apple's trade-secrets lawsuit against OpenAI could stall the startup's hardware ambitions for years, argues M.G. Siegler, tangling the Jony Ive-led device effort in litigation. The piece frames it as another messy split for OpenAI, adding legal risk to a hardware push that was already years from shipping a consumer product.

title: The face on an AI interviewer may matter as much as the decision it makes
blurb: Researchers found that people rejected by an AI job interviewer judged the process as less fair depending on the race and gender of the photorealistic avatar delivering the decision, even though the outcome was identical for everyone. Candidates who matched the avatar on only one trait rated it least fair, suggesting companies auditing AI hiring for algorithmic bias should also test how the interface itself shapes perceived fairness.

title: Muse Spark 1.1
blurb: Meta Superintelligence Labs released Muse Spark 1.1, a multimodal reasoning model built for agentic tasks like planning, tool use, and computer control across apps, with a context window up to 1 million tokens. It is available now in Thinking mode inside the Meta AI app and via a new OpenAI-compatible Meta Model API in public preview, positioned to run real coding workloads at scale.

title: The FCC’s latest crackdown could put more than DJI drones at risk in the US
blurb: The FCC is penalizing alleged DJI front companies, threatening US availability of not just drones but Osmo cameras and wireless creator gear.

title: How to Evaluate General-Purpose Robot Policies for Real-World Deployment
blurb: NVIDIA introduces a method for evaluating general-purpose robot policies that addresses the field's core testing failures: training and evaluation data drawn from the same visual source, benchmark saturation on static task sets, and binary scores that don't explain why a policy failed. The approach targets scalable simulation-based evaluation with diagnostic feedback and statistical confidence measures rather than a single success rate over N rollouts.

title: Current AI market dynamics point to frontier models becoming commodity infrastructure as the token crunch eases, with value shifting to products built on top (Benedict Evans)
blurb: Benedict Evans argues that frontier models are on track to become commodity infrastructure as the token shortage eases, collapsing the pricing power that currently separates the leading labs. The strategic consequence is that durable value migrates up the stack to the products and workflows built on top of the models, not the models themselves.

title: Mesh LLM: distributed AI computing on iroh
blurb: Mesh LLM pools GPUs and memory across the machines you already have and exposes them as a single OpenAI-compatible API, built on iroh endpoints. It serves each request one of three ways: locally, routed to a peer that has the model loaded, or split across several machines for models too large for one box, and ships a catalog of 40+ models ranging from sub-billion-parameter models to 235B mixture-of-experts giants.

title: What xAI's Grok Build CLI Actually Sends to xAI
blurb: A reproducible teardown of xAI's official Grok Build CLI shows that, on a normal consumer login, the tool uploads the entire repository, including full git history and files the agent was explicitly told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces. On a 12 GB repo of never-read files, the storage channel moved 5.10 GiB versus 192 KB on the model turn, and disabling "Improve the model" left trace_upload_enabled set to true.

title: 'Ghostcommit' hides prompt injection in images to fool AI agents, steal secrets
blurb: Researchers demonstrated Ghostcommit, a prompt injection attack that hides malicious instructions inside a PNG that text-based AI code reviewers never open. An AGENTS.md convention file points to the image, whose rendered text tells a coding agent to read .env byte by byte and emit the keys as a module constant. CodeRabbit's default config excludes images, Bugbot found nothing, and in one run Cursor driving Claude Sonnet exfiltrated secrets on the first try. The researchers note 73% of merged PRs reached the default branch with no substantive review.

title: Exclusive: Early 30-second AI videos generated by Seedance 2.5
blurb: ByteDance is preparing to release Seedance 2.5, the first model in its video line to generate native 30-second clips in a single pass, with an API release now targeted for July 16. The model accepts up to 50 reference inputs spanning images, video, and audio, supports region-level frame edits, and will roll out through Jimeng, Dreamina, CapCut, and the Volcano Engine API.

title: How members of the extremist group Boko Haram are using AI chatbots to design explosives, fix or upgrade weapons, and brainstorm attack ideas (New York Times)
blurb: A New York Times investigation found that members of Boko Haram are using consumer AI chatbots to design explosives, repair and upgrade weapons, and brainstorm attack plans. The report adds to mounting evidence that extremist groups are adapting general-purpose LLMs to operational uses that safety guardrails were meant to block.

title: AI found an Ethereum bug that could take validators offline, but humans had to prove it
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation pointed coordinated AI agents at the gossipsub messaging system its validators rely on and found a remotely triggerable crash, now fixed and disclosed as CVE-2026-34219. The harder problem was triage: the agents produced detailed, confident findings for test-only crashes, infeasible attacks, and trivial proofs that were not bugs. Because agents miss exploits that unfold over valid steps, the Foundation now uses them to propose suspicious sequences while leaving traditional testing and human review to confirm them.

title: Nvidia, CoreWeave, and Nebius: Inside the Circular Financing of the GPU Boom
blurb: Neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius have ridden hyperscaler demand to soaring revenue, backlog, and share prices, but this analysis argues the buildout rests on shaky financing. Both have contracted 3.5 GW of power while running unprofitable, cash-thin operations funded by GPU-backed debt. The bigger risk is circular financing, where Nvidia's own investments and backstopping prop up the customers buying its chips.

title: Inside the Secret Factory That Supplies Ukraine’s War Drones
blurb: German startup Helsing SE mass-produces inexpensive AI-powered strike drones that supply Ukraine's war effort, illustrating a broader shift in how military spending flows toward cheap autonomous weapons. The company's factory model prioritizes volume and low unit cost over the expensive, bespoke hardware that has traditionally defined defense procurement.

title: OpenAI has folded safety into research again. Its head of safety is leaving.
blurb: OpenAI merged its safety and research teams under a single leader, expanding Mia Glaese's role to VP of Research and Safety, and head of safety systems Johannes Heidecke is leaving as a result. It is the second reorganization of the safety group into a research-led structure in under two years, following the dissolution of the Superalignment, AGI Readiness, and Mission Alignment teams.

title: A Top OpenAI Executive, Fidji Simo, Steps Down
blurb: Fidji Simo, Sam Altman's second in command and one of OpenAI's most senior executives, is stepping down and will shift to a part-time advisory role after taking medical leave in April. Her departure removes a key operational deputy from Altman's inner circle as OpenAI scales its consumer and enterprise businesses.

title: Apple Sues OpenAI Over AI Talent Fight
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI, alleging former Apple employees improperly took trade secrets as OpenAI expands its device ambitions. The suit reflects an intensifying fight for AI talent and hardware leadership, with SK Hynix separately warning that AI memory demand will outpace supply and planning to raise its US investment beyond $35B.

title: Meta killed its Muse Image AI feature three days after launch. Hollywood had had enough.
blurb: Meta pulled its Muse Image AI feature from Instagram and the Meta AI app three days after launch, saying it "missed the mark" on privacy after backlash from SAG-AFTRA, talent agency CAA, and actors including Hannah Einbinder. The tool, the first release from Meta Superintelligence Labs, let anyone generate images referencing any public Instagram profile's photos, with users opted in by default rather than asked to consent.

title: How AI Advice Is Undermining Eating-Disorder Therapy
blurb: This WSJ investigation finds that AI chatbots dispensing plausible nutrition and fitness advice can reinforce disordered eating and undercut eating-disorder treatment. Even models trained on legitimate research can hand users reasonable-sounding guidance that becomes dangerous for those in recovery, exposing a gap between generic wellness answers and clinical care.

title: Apple's OpenAI lawsuit follows months of simmering tensions and highlights OpenAI's hardware chief Tang Tan's strained relationship with former boss John Ternus (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple sued OpenAI amid months of escalating tensions between the two companies, according to a Bloomberg report from Mark Gurman. The dispute reportedly centers in part on the strained relationship between OpenAI's hardware chief Tang Tan and his former Apple boss John Ternus, underscoring how the AI hardware talent war has spilled into open legal conflict.

title: Forget typosquatting; slopsquatting is the software supply chain threat created by AI coding tools
blurb: Slopsquatting is an emerging software supply chain attack that weaponizes LLM hallucinations, where AI coding assistants invent plausible-sounding package names that attackers then register and fill with malware. Unlike typosquatting, registries have no defense against it at scale, and the malicious packages can sit undetected in production for months or years while injecting code across countless environments.

title: US software development job postings on Indeed have grown by ~15% since the launch of Claude Code in February 2025, while overall job postings fell by 7% (Guillermo Gallacher/Indeed Hiring Lab)
blurb: US software development job postings on Indeed have grown roughly 15% since Claude Code launched in February 2025, while overall postings fell 7% over the same period. The Indeed Hiring Lab data cuts against the assumption that coding agents are shrinking developer demand, though it does not establish whether Claude Code or broader market forces drove the divergence.

title: Researchers hid a prompt injection inside a PNG, and AI fell for it
blurb: Security researchers demonstrated a prompt injection attack that hides malicious instructions inside PNG image files, which AI code review tools typically treat as decorative assets and skip inspecting. The payload lies dormant until a developer later asks an AI coding assistant an unrelated task, at which point the absorbed instructions can access sensitive files and disguise exfiltrated data as ordinary-looking code values.

title: OpenAI's head of safety is reportedly leaving as part of company reorganization
blurb: OpenAI's head of safety systems, Johannes Heidecke, is reportedly leaving as part of a reorganization that folds the safety teams under Mia Glaese, the company's new VP of research and safety. Saachi Jain will serve as interim head of safety systems, and chief research officer Mark Chen framed the shift as integrating safety work more directly into frontier-model development and launch decisions.

title: OpenAI Engineer’s ‘LOL’ Moment Set Stage for Legal Fight With Apple
blurb: Apple sued former iPhone engineer Chang Liu, alleging he took confidential material with him when he left for OpenAI's new hardware division. Apple's complaint centers on a "LOL" message Liu allegedly sent, which the company cites as evidence he knowingly downloaded proprietary files before departing.

title: Ghost Font: A font that humans can read but AI cannot
blurb: Ghost Font renders text as motion that humans read but leading AI models can't decode from static frames.

title: Apple's lawsuit could sidetrack OpenAI's hardware aspirations for years, or possibly forever, as the startup gets into yet another controversy and messy divorce (M.G. Siegler/Spyglass)
blurb: Apple filed a lawsuit against OpenAI that could stall the startup's hardware ambitions for years, according to M.G. Siegler. The author argues the legal fight adds to a growing list of controversies and partnership breakdowns that may jeopardize OpenAI's push into consumer devices, potentially delaying its hardware plans indefinitely.

title: Zhipu’s Chinese Founder Says Frontier AI Should Stay Open to All
blurb: Zhipu founder Zhang Peng argued that frontier AI should stay broadly accessible to everyone rather than being controlled by a select few, entering an intensifying debate over the risks of increasingly capable models. The position aligns with the open-weight release strategy that has distinguished Chinese labs from their more closed US counterparts.

title: US software development job postings on Indeed have grown by ~15% since the launch of Claude Code in February 2025, while overall job postings fell by 7% (Guillermo Gallacher/Indeed Hiring Lab)
blurb: US software development job postings on Indeed have grown about 15% since Claude Code launched in February 2025, even as overall postings fell 7% over the same stretch. That cuts against the assumption that coding agents shrink engineering headcount. So far the tooling looks like it is expanding demand for developers who can direct it, not replacing the roles outright, and the gap between software and the broader job market is widening rather than closing.

title: Current AI market dynamics point to frontier models becoming commodity infrastructure as the token crunch eases, with value shifting to products built on top (Benedict Evans)
blurb: Frontier models are sliding toward commodity infrastructure as the token crunch eases, which means the raw capability everyone paid a premium for is becoming a utility. When the model layer stops being scarce, the differentiation moves up the stack to the products, workflows, and data built on top. The winners in that shift aren't the labs racing to the frontier, but the companies that own the customer, the proprietary data, and the workflow people actually live in.

title: Nvidia, CoreWeave, and Nebius: Inside the Circular Financing of the GPU Boom
blurb: Neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius have turned quick access to Nvidia GPUs into soaring sales, backlog, and share prices, but the buildout is funded by GPU-backed debt, hyperscaler contracts, and Nvidia's own equity. Both have locked up 3.5 GW of contracted power while most of it isn't live yet, and converting that backlog into revenue is expensive against thin cash flow and rising debt. The circular part is Nvidia investing in and backstopping the same customers buying its chips, which props up demand and makes the surge harder to read.

title: The EU says autoplay and infinite scroll are illegal. Meta has until it responds to disagree.
blurb: The EU accused Meta of engineering Facebook and Instagram to be addictive, ordering it to kill autoplay and infinite scroll by default or face fines up to 6% of global revenue, roughly $12 billion against its 2025 turnover. The case targets architecture, not content, arguing that engagement-maxed feeds and frictionless design push users into compulsive scrolling. For any founder whose growth model leans on autoplay, infinite feeds, or engagement-tuned recommendations, Brussels is turning core retention mechanics into regulatory liability, and Teen Accounts-style opt-in controls won't clear the bar.

title: One Way to Build a Great AI Agent: Just Start With a Dashboard, Then Add the Agent
blurb: Build the dashboard first, then add the agentic layer on top. SaaStr has shipped more than 20 production agents, and the ones they built themselves all started as a plain dashboard that structured the data before any agent touched it. Their AI VP of Customer Success began as a vibe-coded sponsor portal that just assigned tasks and sent reminders, then grew into a system that cut human hours by more than 70% and drove on-time submissions up more than 10x. The dashboard is what makes real personalization possible, because an agent needs current, structured data to trigger and act on.

title: Mesh LLM: distributed AI computing on iroh
blurb: Mesh LLM pools GPUs and memory across machines you already own and exposes them as a single OpenAI-compatible API at localhost:9337, routing each request to run locally, on a peer that has the model loaded, or split across boxes as a pipeline. The pluggable runtime ships a catalog of 40+ models spanning from sub-billion-parameter models that fit on a laptop to 235B mixture-of-experts giants, communicating over iroh endpoints.

title: We scaled PgBouncer to 4x throughput
blurb: ClickHouse ran a fleet of PgBouncer processes behind one port using so_reuseport, letting the kernel load-balance connections across all 16 cores instead of pinning connection pooling to a single core. On identical 16-vCPU EC2 hardware, the fleet hit roughly 336k transactions/sec versus 87k for one process, about 4x, and used peering to forward query-cancel requests to whichever process actually owns the session.

title: What xAI's Grok Build CLI Actually Sends to xAI
blurb: A researcher's teardown of xAI's official Grok Build CLI shows that on a normal consumer login it uploads the entire repository, including full git history and files the agent was explicitly told not to read, to a Google Cloud Storage bucket named grok-code-session-traces via POST /v1/storage. Findings are backed by captured artifacts and SHA-256 hashes, and the author notes the upload stays active by default even after disabling "Improve the model.

title: Compromised jscrambler 8.14.0 npm Release Drops Rust Infostealer During Install
blurb: The jscrambler npm package's 8.14.0 release was compromised, shipping a preinstall hook that drops a Rust infostealer for Windows, macOS, and Linux and runs it during install. The payload sweeps for AWS, Azure, and GCP credentials, crypto wallets, browser secrets, and config files for AI coding tools like Claude Desktop, Cursor, and Zed, with the Linux build able to load an eBPF program into the kernel.

title: 'Ghostcommit' hides prompt injection in images to fool AI agents, steal secrets
blurb: Researchers demonstrated "Ghostcommit," an attack that hides prompt-injection instructions inside a PNG so text-based AI code reviewers like CodeRabbit and Bugbot skip the image entirely and merge a poisoned AGENTS.md convention file. A coding agent later follows the pointer, reads .env byte by byte, and writes the repo's secrets into source as an innocuous integer constant, exploiting a review gap where 73% of merged PRs reach the default branch with no substantive human or bot review.

title: Your code is fast if you're lucky
blurb: The author found that Clang only compiles a sort's comparison into branch-free conditional-move instructions when the code is written in a specific style, so functionally identical implementations can differ sharply in speed. The post uses a branchless Quicksort built on sorting networks to show which patterns the compiler reliably vectorizes, and why relying on the optimizer to guess your intent leaves performance to chance.

title: ZeroFS vs. Amazon S3 Files
blurb: ZeroFS and Amazon S3 Files both mount a POSIX filesystem on top of object storage, but they invert the bucket's role: S3 Files (built on EFS) keeps a one-file, one-object mapping so images/cat.jpg exports back to the same S3 key, while ZeroFS packs compressed, client-side-encrypted extents into opaque immutable segments. The trade-off is direct S3 interoperability, since a ZeroFS pathname can never be fetched with GetObject and recovery requires the encryption password.

title: UPI: Anatomy of a Payment Transaction
blurb: UPI processed more than 2,272 crore payments in June 2026, and this walkthrough traces a single transaction through the seven separate organizations that clear it between the QR scan and the green tick. Each stop covers what that layer checks and where it can fail, starting with the app, a Third-Party Application Provider that collects your intent and PIN but never touches your money or holds a banking license.

title: Networking and the Internet, from First Principles
blurb: This post explains how the internet works from first principles, tracing a message from Wi-Fi radio waves through copper, fiber, and undersea glass to the software on the other end. It frames packet switching, TCP, DNS, and TLS not as a finished blueprint but as successive patches for concrete limitations, each deployed decades after the network already worked.

title: Show HN: Ant – A JavaScript runtime and ecosystem
blurb: Ant is a new JavaScript runtime that bundles its own toolchain and package ecosystem, positioning itself against Node.js, Deno, and Bun rather than slotting into the existing npm stack. The Show HN pitch is an integrated environment instead of a runtime plus a pile of separate tools, though adopting it means betting on a young ecosystem outside the established npm compatibility path.

title: A pure scheme web programming tool
blurb: Goeteia is a self-hosted Scheme-to-WebAssembly compiler that ships as a ~70 KB gzipped Wasm blob and recompiles source in the browser in about 15 ms per Run, with no server involved. It handles fixnums as unboxed i31refs, does every tail call as a return_call so a 100M-iteration loop runs in constant stack in ~150 ms, and includes a reactive web stack for DOM, Three.js, raw WebGL, and s-expression RPC.

title: Prefer strict tables in SQLite
blurb: SQLite's STRICT tables enforce rigid column typing, rejecting inserts and updates that store the wrong datatype, such as text in an INTEGER column, while still allowing lossless conversions like '123' to an integer. Appending STRICT to a table definition also blocks bogus column types like DATETIME, JSON, or UUID at creation time, catching typos and misconceptions about SQLite's supported datatypes.

title: Show HN: Learn by rebuilding Redis, Git, a database from scratch
blurb: ShipThatCode offers 80+ build-from-scratch courses that teach systems like Redis, Git, databases, and container runtimes by having you implement them in one of nine languages, with each lesson structured as choose, write, then run. The platform also bundles courses into sequenced career paths for roles including backend, frontend, DevOps, and data science, and the free tier requires no credit card.

title: Fake Go DNS scanner spread malware through over 200 GitHub repos — 'Operation Muck and Load' has published 700 malicious modules since January
blurb: Socket traced a Go module posing as a DNS and subdomain scanner to a network of 222 GitHub repositories across 190 accounts, where it acted as a first-stage Windows malware loader staging Vidar, RATs, and XMRig miners. Dubbed "Operation Muck and Load," the campaign published over 700 malicious versions since January before the Go security team blocked it from the module proxy.

title: Prefer strict tables in SQLite
blurb: SQLite's STRICT tables enforce rigid column typing, rejecting inserts and updates that put the wrong datatype in a column instead of silently coercing them the way default tables do. The author argues they should be the default choice: appending STRICT to a table definition also blocks bogus column types like JSON, UUID, or DATETIME at creation time, while still allowing lossless conversions such as '123' into an INTEGER column.

title: We scaled PgBouncer to 4x throughput
blurb: ClickHouse ran a fleet of PgBouncer processes bound to the same port with so_reuseport, letting the kernel load-balance connections across all cores instead of pinning connection pooling to a single one. On a 16-vCPU box, this pushed throughput from a single process's 87k transactions/sec peak to roughly 336k, about 4x. The hard part was query cancellation: cancel requests arrive on new connections the kernel can route to the wrong process, so the pooler processes peer with each other to forward cancels to the session's real owner.

title: Ghost Font: A font that humans can read but AI cannot
blurb: Ghost Font is an experimental "anti-AI" typeface that renders text as motion, encoding each letter in the movement of dots so a human eye reads the message while a single paused frame or screenshot dissolves into static noise. Built as a browser prototype that runs locally and exports a shareable video clip, it revives designer Sang Mun's 2013 ZXX concept for an era where leading models decode OCR-resistant fonts with a single prompt.

title: How a designer created the "dirty, organic, insectoid logo" for Marvel Cosmic Invasion
blurb: Designer Stephane Perez (Mardoch) broke down how he built the Marvel Cosmic Invasion logo, splitting the wordmark into a retro-futuristic COSMIC drawn from '60s and '70s sci-fi title fonts and a raw, insectoid INVASION tied to the antagonist Annihilus. The write-up is a concrete look at treating a logo as narrative, with iteration passes and texture choices tied directly to the game's theme.

title: Hackers Weaponize Balochistan Police Portal in Multi-Group Espionage Campaigns
blurb: SentinelOne disclosed a sustained cyber espionage campaign against Pakistani law enforcement, including Balochistan Police servers holding biometric, criminal, and personnel records, run by suspected China- and India-aligned actors from February 2024 to April 2026. Four clusters deployed PlugX, ShadowPad, Cobalt Strike, and Remcos RAT, with one China-nexus actor planting a custom implant disguised as an update to the citizen-facing Complaint Management System.

title: Lending protocol Bonzo loses 77% of value locked as $9 million oracle exploit rattles Hedera
blurb: An attacker drained roughly $9.05 million from Bonzo Lend, a lending protocol on Hedera, by exploiting a verification flaw in a third-party Supra oracle contract to submit a manipulated SAUCE price update and borrow far beyond deposited collateral. Bonzo's total value locked fell 77% and Hedera's dropped nearly 40% within 24 hours, though a white-hat wallet that borrowed about $1 million during the incident has offered to return the funds.

title: How members of the extremist group Boko Haram are using AI chatbots to design explosives, fix or upgrade weapons, and brainstorm attack ideas (New York Times)
blurb: The New York Times reported that members of Boko Haram are using consumer AI chatbots to design explosives, repair and upgrade weapons, and brainstorm attack plans. The activity underscores the limits of model guardrails against jailbroken or open-weight tooling, raising pressure on vendors and regulators to address dual-use misuse by designated terrorist organizations.

title: 'Ghostcommit' hides prompt injection in images to fool AI agents, steal secrets
blurb: Researchers disclosed "Ghostcommit," an attack that hides prompt-injection instructions inside a PNG so AI code reviewers, which treat images as opaque binary blobs, wave the malicious pull request through. Once merged, an AGENTS.md convention points a coding agent to the image, which instructs it to read .env and emit the repository's secrets as an encoded integer constant; CodeRabbit, Bugbot, and Cursor driving Claude Sonnet all missed or executed it.

title: Fake Go DNS scanner spread malware through over 200 GitHub repos — 'Operation Muck and Load' has published 700 malicious modules since January
blurb: Socket traced a fake Go DNS scanner that acts as a first-stage Windows loader to 222 GitHub repositories across 190 accounts, part of a campaign it tracks as "Operation Muck and Load" that has pushed over 700 malicious module versions since January. The loader runs hidden PowerShell to pull certutil-decoded payloads (Vidar, RATs, XMRig) from dead drops on Pastebin, Telegram, and YouTube; Go's security team has blocked the module from its proxy.

title: Compromised jscrambler 8.14.0 npm Release Drops Rust Infostealer During Install
blurb: A compromised jscrambler 8.14.0 npm release, pushed July 11 under a legitimate maintainer account and absent from the project's GitHub source, uses a preinstall hook to drop and run a native Rust infostealer built for Windows, macOS, and Linux. The payload sweeps cloud credentials, crypto wallets, browser secrets, and AI coding-tool configs holding API keys and MCP credentials, and on Linux can load an eBPF program directly into the kernel.

title: Ghost Accounts Abuse GitHub API in Mass Recon Campaign
blurb: Threat actors are abusing GitHub's largely unauthenticated API to enumerate organizations, members, and repositories, using over 50 dormant "ghost" accounts registered two to five years ago, Datadog reports. The recon requests return HTTP 200s with no auth-failure signals and blend with normal traffic, but some campaigns escalated to cloning repos and exfiltrating data, so defenders should hunt for anomalous user-agent naming on actions touching private repositories.

title: Forget typosquatting; slopsquatting is the software supply chain threat created by AI coding tools
blurb: Slopsquatting is a supply-chain attack that weaponizes LLM hallucinations: AI coding assistants invent plausible-sounding package names, attackers register those names on public registries and populate them with malware. Because these are novel invented names rather than misspellings of real libraries, registry typosquatting defenses like blocking "crossenv" against "cross-env" don't catch them. Defenders should pin and vet AI-suggested dependencies before install, and validate that every package an assistant recommends actually exists and is legitimately maintained.

title: Researchers hid a prompt injection inside a PNG, and AI fell for it
blurb: Researchers demonstrated a prompt-injection attack that embeds malicious instructions inside a PNG committed via pull request, evading AI code-review tools that treat images as decorative assets. The payload lies dormant until a developer later invokes an AI coding assistant on an unrelated task, at which point it exfiltrates sensitive project files as innocuous-looking values in generated code. Susceptibility varied by assistant wrapper, not the underlying model.

title: The Hotel Upgrade Hack: Mass Assignment Vulnerabilities in APIs
blurb: Mass assignment vulnerabilities let attackers set fields the server never intended to expose by adding extra keys to an API request, binding user input directly to internal models. The canonical case is Egor Homakov's 2012 GitHub exploit, where he injected an organization_id into a profile-update POST to add himself to the rails/rails org without approval. Defenders should enforce explicit allow-lists for bindable fields (Rails strong parameters, DTO schemas, or per-endpoint field whitelists) rather than passing req.body straight into create or update calls.

title: Australia warns of global campaign targeting vulnerable CMS platforms
blurb: Australia's ACSC warned that a global campaign is exploiting known CMS and plugin vulnerabilities across WordPress, Craft CMS, Joomla JCE, and others to deploy webshells, with many small- and medium-sized Australian businesses already compromised. Patch affected CMS, themes, and plugins, remove unused components, make web directories read-only, and monitor for unauthorized file creation and unexpected child-process spawning.

title: Irish datacenters now guzzle 23% of the country's electricity
blurb: Datacenters consumed 23% of Ireland's total electricity in 2025, up from 5% a decade ago, as hyperscale AI and cloud buildouts strain a grid already warning of supply shortfalls. Enterprises running Irish capacity face tightening constraints, with the grid operator pausing new Dublin-area connections and pushing operators toward on-site generation and stricter efficiency terms.

title: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 becomes the preferred model in Microsoft 365 Copilot
blurb: Microsoft made OpenAI's GPT-5.6 the default model across Microsoft 365 Copilot, wiring the new series into Word, Excel, and PowerPoint on day one. The move reaffirms the OpenAI partnership as Microsoft's primary Copilot engine over its own in-house and Anthropic models, keeping enterprise Copilot buyers on OpenAI capabilities without any added configuration or licensing change.

title: We scaled PgBouncer to 4x throughput
blurb: ClickHouse's Managed Postgres team describes running a fleet of PgBouncer processes bound to the same port with so_reuseport, since PgBouncer is single-threaded and pins one core no matter how many the box has. On a 16-vCPU c7i.4xlarge, the fleet hit roughly 336k transactions/sec against a single process's 87k peak, about 4x. The write-up covers the operational catch: cancel requests can land on the wrong process, fixed by peering, with the connection budget split across the fleet so Postgres is never oversubscribed.

title: Mesh LLM: distributed AI computing on iroh
blurb: Mesh LLM pools GPUs across the machines a team already owns and exposes them as one OpenAI-compatible API, routing each request to run locally, hand off to a peer with the model loaded, or split a too-large model across several boxes as a pipeline. Built on iroh endpoints with a pluggable plugin architecture, it ships a catalog of 40+ models spanning laptop-sized 500M weights up to 235B mixture-of-experts giants.

title: ZeroFS vs. Amazon S3 Files
blurb: ZeroFS and Amazon S3 Files both expose POSIX filesystems over object storage, but split on whether the bucket stays usable as plain S3. S3 Files preserves one-file, one-object identity and exports asynchronously after 60 seconds of inactivity, while ZeroFS packs, compresses, and client-side encrypts extents into opaque segments, trading direct S3 access for cheaper small-file writes.

title: Hedera lending protocol Bonzo Lend hit for $9 million after Supra verifier accepts manipulated price update
blurb: Bonzo Lend, a lending protocol on Hedera, lost $9 million after an attacker fed a manipulated price update through a Supra oracle verifier that accepted it without rejecting the forged data. A second wallet borrowed roughly $1 million during the exploit but identified itself as a white hat and said it would return the funds.

title: Bitcoin, ether ETFs snap eight-week outflow streaks with $282 million combined inflow
blurb: Bitcoin and ether ETFs recorded a combined $282 million in weekly inflows, ending eight-week outflow streaks that had drained $9.46 billion from the two groups. The rebound recovered only about 3% of those losses, leaving net positioning well below where it stood before the eight-week drawdown began.

title: AI found an Ethereum bug that could take validators offline, but humans had to prove it
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation ran coordinated AI agents against its gossipsub messaging layer and surfaced a remotely triggerable crash that could knock validator nodes offline, now patched and disclosed as CVE-2026-34219. Most of the effort went to human triage rather than discovery, as the agents produced convincing false positives around test-only crashes and infeasible attacks, so the Foundation now uses agents to propose suspicious sequences and keeps traditional testing and human review to validate them.

title: Lending protocol Bonzo loses 77% of value locked as $9 million oracle exploit rattles Hedera
blurb: Bonzo Lend, a decentralized lending protocol on Hedera, lost roughly $9.05 million after an attacker exploited a verification flaw in a third-party Supra oracle, depositing near-worthless SAUCE tokens and submitting a manipulated price update to borrow far beyond their collateral. Bonzo's TVL fell 77% and Hedera's network-wide TVL dropped nearly 40% in the 24 hours after the exploit, leaving the chain at $25.7 million locked.

title: Bitcoin’s BIP 110 fork deadline nears with miner support at zero
blurb: Bitcoin's BIP-110, the Reduced Data Temporary Soft Fork, faces an early August activation deadline with miner support still below 1% and node adoption stuck in the low single digits. The proposal would cap OP_RETURN and block most arbitrary data pushes above 256 bytes for one year, but with Michael Saylor and Adam Back opposed, it now looks headed for a small minority chain rather than a network-wide change.

title: Ripple once weighed shutting down and handing XRP to shareholders, CEO says
blurb: Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse said the company seriously considered shutting down and distributing its XRP holdings to shareholders on a pro rata basis rather than fighting the SEC's 2020 lawsuit. Ripple instead spent about $150 million over four years to litigate, eventually winning a ruling that XRP itself is not a security and settling the case in 2025.

title: Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paul Grewal to Step Down
blurb: Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paul Grewal notified the company he will step down effective July 31, 2026, per an 8-K filing, with legal VP Molly Abraham expected to succeed him as general counsel and secretary. Grewal joined in 2020 and became the public face of Coinbase's regulatory fights, including its multiyear SEC litigation over unregistered securities and its ongoing CLARITY Act advocacy in Washington.

title: The most underrated token type
blurb: Arcade tokens are digital currencies meant to be spent inside a specific app or product ecosystem, with supply and demand managed to hold a relatively stable value. This a16z post frames them as the on-chain version of airline miles, credit card points, and in-game gold, but open, interoperable, and composable rather than siloed in a company database. It covers where their value comes from, how builders can deploy them, and the design tradeoffs involved.

title: Crypto IPO market stalls as capital rotates to AI and macro uncertainty weighs
blurb: The crypto IPO pipeline has stalled as capital rotates into AI and macro uncertainty erodes appetite for new listings, according to Cohen & Company's Christian Lopez, who cites funding constraints and investor caution over regulation as the cause. Kraken parent Payward, Consensys, Ledger, and Grayscale have all delayed offerings after weak post-listing performances from Circle, Bullish, and BitGo cooled the 2026 outlook.

title: UPI: Anatomy of a Payment Transaction
blurb: UPI processed more than 2,272 crore payments in June 2026, more than any other real-time payment system in the world, yet the app you tap is only the first of seven parties in the chain and never touches your money. This piece traces a single transaction from scan to green tick, detailing what each layer checks and where it fails. It also shows the app tier stays a duopoly, with PhonePe and Google Pay together carrying roughly four-fifths of volume.

title: Banks’ Record Bond Short Sparks Quest for Answers
blurb: Primary dealers have flipped to a record net short on US Treasuries, the first time these Wall Street banks have collectively bet against debt they historically warehoused in bulk. The positioning is forcing investors to reexamine who absorbs new supply as the Fed unwinds its balance sheet, raising questions about how deep buyer demand runs if dealers no longer backstop the market.
